title: run buildah bud tests using podman
body:
Proof of concept, guaranteed to fail in CI (if it even makes it
as far as CI, which I doubt, because my .cirrus.yml changes
are a wild guess.)

Checking in just to get the conversation started.

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body:
What the heck was that about? (The 'Check Automation' failure with golang-ci-lint)? Did someone re-run it manually, or did it restart automatically? 

In any case, here's the new diagram. New step is dark purple near center right.
![cirrus-map-test_buildah_bud](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/265639/107437180-d5b1f300-6aeb-11eb-86c6-9d90485ea3f7.png)

Hey, it worked! (Well, "it ran!"): [log](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/6651056966860800/html/bud-podman-fedora-33-root-host.log.html) [the remote test is hanging, no useful info in log, and I'm not going to stay up to wait]

The `--dns` failure (32) looks real. The symlink ones (78-81) are way over my head, might merit a look-see from someone knowledgeable. Some of the others are exit-code or error-message differences, I can work around those. (I'm thinking of adding a generic rule to change expected-status-code `2` to `125`; will try that tomorrow).

Some of the `skips`, as I found out by looking at the sources, happen because of a test like this:
```
   ...
   buildah do something
   buildah from that-image
}
```
That is: all the interesting stuff happens, then there's a final `buildah from` that doesn't actually do anything, simply confirms that the image exists. I need to cogitate on that because I have a hunch there's a simple way to detect or fix those; we could then greatly improve our coverage.
podman image exists? replace podman from  for those use cases?

I'm actually leaning toward some sort of `podman run` + `podman commit` combination, because in reviewing `bud.bats` I see a lot of cases like ``` buildah from buildah run ``` It's getting complicated really fast, though.

I had a tiny insight and decided to simply use `buildah` itself for everything other than `bud`: that is, convert `buildah bud` to `podman build`, and keep everything else the same (`buildah from`, `inspect`, `images`, `run`). [Result](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5433052291661824/html/bud-podman-fedora-33-root-host.log.html). Pass count is now 387, with 94 failures which I'll now start looking at.

Good news: given enough `skips`, we have [green tests](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5780713858400256/html/bud-podman-fedora-33-root-host.log.html). (Issues filed for the majority of those skips, and I expect most of those to be fixed).

Less good news: the `podman-remote build` work is really hard, and I keep running into snags (most recent one: dealing with the `--root` option which is a deeply ingrained part of buildah tests). How necessary is it to get these tests running under podman-remote?
I am fine with skipping them for now.  The amount of bugs this effort has revealed thus far has been great.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *edsantiago*, *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago,rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
This is a huge increase of test coverage on podman build, nice work @edsantiago I think we should work to get some of these fixes back into buildah to make the tests easier to maintain.

@rhatdan @edsantiago Thought:  Maintaining these diffs will compound over time as podman and buildah branch.  Since updates to release branches do happen on occasion, it may become difficult for non-experts (in these tests) to produce working diffs for their changes and/or vendor updates.  This may unduly impact important security-related PRs (i.e. where these tests failing, blocks the merge).

Perhaps it would help to provide a `.cirrus.yml` "escape hatch" for this task, [similar to what we do for the static build task.](https://github.com/edsantiago/libpod/blob/3baa9da4edec72a70aec4aa25a29b4afd82ed689/.cirrus.yml#L330-L332).  A simple thing like that, where someone can un-comment it in a panic/emergency situation, to be addressed after the critical security change is merged.

@cevich thanks for the reminder. I had wanted to include a bypass, but wanted to do it via a mechanism like `[CI:DOCS]` where the bypass is in the PR comments. Do you have a suggestion on how to do that? IIRC Cirrus yaml doesn't allow adding conditions (like `&& $CIRRUS_CHANGE_TITLE !=~ '.*SKIP:BUILDAH.*'`) to a star-anchor thing.
```
# allow_failures: $CI == $CI
```
This makes me uncomfortable because it's essentially the same as removing the entire buildah_bud task, except kind of wishy-washy: it's saying "we will never get around to fixing this". If that's the case, I think it's much better to rip out the buildah_bud task entirely.

> IIRC Cirrus yaml doesn't allow adding conditions

Cirrus-CI allows this, we simply don't do it because adding it to work with the `*.CI:DOCS.*` feature, makes human-brains explode.

> `# allow_failures: $CI == $CI`

This is intended as temporary mechanism and reminder: "This task may become unstable, it's okay to skip it in extraordinary circumstances **but** if you do, please also open an issue."

Realistically, we're only running the full set of tasks on a 3-5 branches IIRC.  As they age, they join the majority of other defunct branches where I end up gutting `.cirrus.yml` down to only the 'smoke' test.  So the risk pool here is large enough to be annoying, but small enough I think having an escape-hatch-via-uncomment is probably fine.

Otherwise, something like this might work:

`    only_if: $CIRRUS_CHANGE_TITLE !=~ '.*CI:DOCS.*' && $CIRRUS_CHANGE_TITLE !=~ '.*CI:NOBUILDAH.*'`

...but it's very brain-hurty for me to read :confused:
